chapter_summary: The chapter describes the degradation of dharma through the four yugas, culminating in Kali Yuga, and emphasizes that constant remembrance and chanting of Krishna's names can purify even the worst sins. It also lists many past kings and demons who were attached to the earth and perished.

story.title: The Degradation of Dharma in the Yugas and the Glory of Krishna's Name
story.arc: Śuka describes the characteristics of each yuga and the power of chanting Krishna's names to overcome Kali's defects.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is set in the context of the Kali Yuga, with references to past yugas and future events.
story.comic_potential: The personification of Earth laughing at kings fighting for her, and the vivid descriptions of Kali Yuga's degradation could be depicted humorously.

teachings.teaching: The earth laughs at kings who fight for her, as they are like playthings of death.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this highlights the futility of external conquest without inner self-mastery. True victory is over the six enemies (kama, krodha, etc.).

teachings.teaching: Kings and demons who were attached to the earth have all perished, leaving only stories.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that attachment to the transient leads to suffering; the only permanent reality is the Self (Atman).

teachings.teaching: Hearing and chanting the glories of the Lord destroys inauspiciousness and bestows devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on constant remembrance (smaraṇa) as a means to purify the mind and attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, the only great quality is that by chanting Krishna's names one becomes free and attains the supreme goal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the essence of the age: the practice of nama-sankirtana is the most effective Kriya-Yoga for this era.

teachings.teaching: The Lord dwelling in the heart removes all sins accumulated over thousands of births when heard, chanted, meditated upon, or worshipped.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the Kriya-Yoga principle that the indwelling divine (Antaryami) purifies the practitioner through inner connection.

chapter_summary: This verse contrasts the methods of worship in the four yugas: in Satya-yuga, meditation on Vishnu; in Treta-yuga, performing sacrifices; in Dvapara-yuga, temple worship; and in Kali-yuga, chanting the holy name of Hari (kirtana).

story.title: The Four Yugas and Their Prescribed Methods of Worship
story.arc: A concise statement on the progressive decline of spiritual practice and the corresponding recommended modes of worship.
story.timeline_note: Refers to the four yugas: Satya, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a didactic verse.

teachings.teaching: In Kali-yuga, the most effective means of attaining the same result as meditation, sacrifice, and worship in previous yugas is simply chanting the holy name of Hari (kirtana).
teachings.lens_note: Emphasizes the power of nama-sankirtana in the current age.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the four types of cosmic dissolution (pralaya): nitya (constant), naimittika (occasional), prākṛtika (elemental), and ātyantika (ultimate). It details the process of elemental dissolution during prākṛtika pralaya, where the universe is reabsorbed into its unmanifest source. The chapter concludes by emphasizing that the only refuge from the cycle of saṃsāra is the constant hearing and chanting of the Lord's pastimes, and traces the lineage of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa from Nārāyaṇa Ṛṣi to Śuka.

story.title: The Four Dissolutions and the Glory of the Bhāgavata
story.arc: Concluding teachings on cosmology and the supreme refuge
story.timeline_note: Post-dynastic, during the Kali-yuga; the chapter describes cyclic time scales from atomic to multi-billion year periods.
story.comic_potential: Visualizing the cosmic dissolution: the sun drinking up waters, fire from Saṅkarṣaṇa's mouth, and the universe shrinking like a burnt cow-dung cake.

teachings.teaching: The universe undergoes periodic dissolutions: naimittika (at the end of Brahmā's day), prākṛtika (at the end of Brahmā's life), and ātyantika (final liberation).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these dissolutions correspond to the yogi's progressive withdrawal of senses, prāṇa, and mind into the Self, culminating in kaivalya.

teachings.teaching: During prākṛtika pralaya, the elements are sequentially dissolved: earth into water, water into fire, fire into air, air into ether, ether into ego, ego into mahat, mahat into unmanifest prakṛti.
teachings.lens_note: This sequence mirrors the yogic process of pratyāhāra and laya, where the yogi reverses creation to merge in the unmanifest source.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate reality (Brahman) is beyond all states (waking, dream, deep sleep) and beyond the guṇas; it is eternal, unmanifest, and the cause of all causes.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the yogi must transcend the three states of consciousness to realize the turīya, the fourth state, which is the Self.

teachings.teaching: The world is a superimposition on the Self, like clouds in the sky; it appears and disappears but the Self remains unchanged.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of viveka: discerning the real from the unreal. In Kriya-Yoga, this is achieved through meditation on the inner light.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is described in various ways by worldly and Vedic people, just as gold is fashioned into many forms.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes that all paths lead to the same truth, but Kriya-Yoga provides a direct method to realize the Lord within.

teachings.teaching: When the ego (ahaṅkāra) is destroyed by the sword of discrimination, one attains the ultimate dissolution (ātyantika pralaya), which is liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This is the goal of Kriya-Yoga: the complete dissolution of the ego through meditation and self-inquiry.

teachings.teaching: The only boat to cross the ocean of saṃsāra is the constant immersion in the pastimes of the Lord (līlā-kathā).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that bhakti and kriyā are complementary; hearing and chanting the Lord's glories purifies the mind and prepares it for meditation.

chapter_summary: Śuka instructs King Parīkṣit on the nature of the self, distinguishing it from the body and mind, and teaches that the self is eternal, unborn, and identical with Brahman. He advises the king to give up the animalistic notion of death and realize his true nature as the supreme self, beyond the gunas and the cycle of birth and death.

story.title: Instruction on the Eternal Self
story.arc: Śuka teaches Parīkṣit the nature of the self, culminating in the realization that the self is beyond death and identical with Brahman.
story.timeline_note: After the curse, Parīkṣit is awaiting death from Takṣaka's bite; Śuka instructs him.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic contrast between the king's imminent death and the teaching of immortality could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The self is unborn, eternal, and not subject to death; the notion of death is a beastly misconception.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the self as the witness (sākṣī), beyond the body-mind complex, achieved through sustained meditative practice.

teachings.teaching: Just as the space in a pot merges with the universal space when the pot breaks, so the individual self merges with Brahman upon death of the body.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that this merging is not a new event but the recognition of an already existing identity, realized through Kriya-Yoga's dissolution of ego.

teachings.teaching: The mind creates bodies, qualities, and actions; from mind arises māyā, causing the cycle of saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that by stilling the mind through prāṇāyāma and meditation, one transcends māyā and attains liberation.

teachings.teaching: The self is self-luminous, beyond the manifest and unmanifest, and is the unchanging support like space.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga experience of the inner light (jyoti) as the witness consciousness.

teachings.teaching: One should contemplate the self as residing within oneself, using intellect pregnant with inference and meditation on Vāsudeva.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret 'Vāsudeva' as the indwelling self, and the contemplation as the Kriya-Yoga technique of inner absorption.

teachings.teaching: Realizing 'I am Brahman, the supreme abode,' one becomes free from fear of death and sees the body and universe as not separate from the self.
teachings.lens_note: This is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga: the direct experience of non-duality, where the practitioner knows oneself as the deathless reality.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit, having heard the Purāṇa from Śuka, attains liberation and is bitten by Takṣaka. His son Janamejaya performs a snake sacrifice to avenge him, but is persuaded by Bṛhaspati to stop. The chapter then describes the origin of the Vedas from Oṃkāra and their division by Vyāsa.

story.title: The Liberation of Parīkṣit and the Origin of the Vedas
story.arc: Parīkṣit attains liberation after hearing the Purāṇa; his death by snake bite leads to Janamejaya's snake sacrifice, which is halted by Bṛhaspati's wisdom. The narrative then shifts to the cosmic origin of sound and the Vedas.
story.timeline_note: After the events of the Mahābhārata, during the Kali Yuga.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic snake sacrifice and the intervention of Indra and Bṛhaspati offer vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: One should not fear death when absorbed in the supreme Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of niṣkāma karma and inner stillness, where the yogi transcends fear by resting in the self.

teachings.teaching: Life and death are determined by one's own karma; no external agent gives happiness or suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes personal responsibility and the law of karma as a foundation for ethical action and self-discipline.

teachings.teaching: The supreme goal is the Viṣṇu-pada, realized by renouncing ego and possessiveness.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's aim of dissolving ahaṃkāra through meditation and service.

teachings.teaching: One should endure insults, not despise anyone, and not create enmity based on the body.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework teaches equanimity and non-reactivity as essential for inner purification.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas originate from Oṃkāra, the sound of Brahman, which is realized through yoga.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, Oṃ is the primordial vibration used in meditation to still the mind and connect with the self.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the transmission of the Vedas by Vyasa to his disciples, the subsequent branching of Vedic recensions, and the story of Yajnavalkya receiving the Yajurveda from the Sun god after a dispute with his guru.

story.title: The Branching of the Vedas and Yajnavalkya's Receiving the Yajurveda
story.arc: Transmission of Vedic knowledge from Vyasa to his disciples and subsequent divisions; Yajnavalkya's conflict with his guru and his acquisition of the Yajurveda from the Sun god.
story.timeline_note: After the Mahabharata war, Vyasa compiled the Vedas and taught them to his disciples, leading to various recensions.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic scene of Yajnavalkya vomiting the Yajus and the Sun god appearing as a horse to give him new Yajus could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Sun god is the inner controller and source of all Vedic knowledge; by worshiping him, one can attain spiritual wisdom.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the practice of Surya-kriya as a means to awaken inner consciousness and receive divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The Vedic tradition is preserved through guru-shishya parampara; even after disputes, knowledge continues through new channels.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights the importance of surrender to the guru and the transformative power of tapasya to overcome obstacles in spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter enumerates the lineage of teachers of the Atharvaveda and the Puranas, defines the ten characteristics of a Purana, and lists the eighteen major Puranas.

story.title: The Lineage of Teachers and the Characteristics of Purāṇas
story.arc: Sūta narrates the disciplic succession of the Atharvaveda and the Purāṇas, defines the ten characteristics of a Purāṇa, and lists the eighteen major Purāṇas.
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes the transmission of Vedic and Purāṇic knowledge through successive generations of sages.
story.comic_potential: The listing of names and characteristics is didactic; visual representation could show a lineage tree or a sage teaching disciples.

teachings.teaching: A Purāṇa is defined by ten characteristics: sarga (creation), visarga (secondary creation), vṛtti (maintenance), rakṣā (protection), antaras (intervals of Manus), vaṃśa (dynasties), vaṃśānucarita (histories of dynasties), saṃsthā (dissolution), hetu (the cause), and apāśraya (the supreme refuge).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these ten characteristics correspond to stages of spiritual evolution: sarga as the initial impulse of practice, visarga as the diversification of techniques, vṛtti as sustained effort, rakṣā as divine protection, antaras as periods of rest, vaṃśa as lineage of teachers, vaṃśānucarita as their teachings, saṃsthā as dissolution of ego, hetu as the inner motive, and apāśraya as surrender to the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: The cause (hetu) is the jīva (individual soul) associated with avidyā (ignorance) and karma (action).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, avidyā is the root cause of bondage; through yogic practices, one transcends karma and realizes the self.

teachings.teaching: The supreme refuge (apāśraya) is Brahman, which underlies all states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep) and is realized when the mind ceases its threefold activity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga leads to the stilling of the mind's vṛttis (modifications), allowing direct experience of Brahman as the substratum of all states.

chapter_summary: Mārkaṇḍeya's penance and victory over death; Indra's attempt to distract him fails; Lord Nara-Nārāyaṇa appears and Mārkaṇḍeya offers a hymn of praise.

story.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Victory over Death and Vision of Nara-Nārāyaṇa
story.arc: Mārkaṇḍeya performs severe penance, conquers death, and is visited by Nara-Nārāyaṇa after Indra's failed distraction.
story.timeline_note: Set after six manvantaras of Mārkaṇḍeya's penance; during the seventh manvantara.
story.comic_potential: Indra's failed attempt to distract Mārkaṇḍeya with celestial beauties and Kāma's arrows bouncing off harmlessly.

teachings.teaching: Through steadfast penance and devotion, one can conquer even death.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the power of sustained kriya practice to transcend the cycle of birth and death.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace is attained by unwavering focus on the Supreme, unaffected by distractions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga purifies the mind, making it impervious to sensory temptations.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord manifests in forms like Nara-Nārāyaṇa to bless devotees.
teachings.lens_note: This appearance corresponds to the realization of the inner Self (Atman) through advanced kriya meditation.

teachings.teaching: True liberation is found only at the lotus feet of the Lord, beyond the three guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga aims at transcending the guṇas through pranayama and meditation, leading to kaivalya.

chapter_summary: Lord Nārāyaṇa, pleased with Mārkaṇḍeya's devotion, grants him a boon. Mārkaṇḍeya asks to see the Lord's māyā. The Lord agrees and departs. Mārkaṇḍeya then experiences a cosmic deluge, wandering alone for eons, until he sees a banyan tree with a baby boy who swallows him, revealing the entire universe within. The baby disappears, and Mārkaṇḍeya finds himself back in his hermitage.

story.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Vision of the Lord's Māyā
story.arc: Mārkaṇḍeya, after receiving a boon from Nārāyaṇa, experiences a cosmic deluge and is swallowed by a baby boy who reveals the entire universe within him, then returns to his hermitage.
story.timeline_note: Set after Mārkaṇḍeya's penance; the deluge episode spans eons.
story.comic_potential: The baby swallowing the sage and showing the universe inside is visually striking and surreal.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is pleased by unwavering devotion, penance, and self-control.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that consistent practice (kriya) and devotion (bhakti) lead to divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's māyā is inscrutable; the universe is a manifestation of the divine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens: The māyā is the creative power of consciousness; experiencing it reveals the non-dual reality.

teachings.teaching: The sage's vision of the universe within the baby illustrates that all existence is contained within the divine.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the microcosm (body) contains the macrocosm; meditation reveals the inner universe.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is both immanent and transcendent, appearing as a baby yet containing all.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework: The divine is accessible in the heart (guhā-śaya) and also beyond form.

chapter_summary: Sage Mārkaṇḍeya, after experiencing the illusory power of Nārāyaṇa, seeks refuge in Him. Lord Rudra and Pārvatī visit Mārkaṇḍeya, who is in deep meditation. Rudra enters his heart and appears within, causing Mārkaṇḍeya to emerge from samādhi. Mārkaṇḍeya worships them and praises Rudra. Rudra offers a boon, and Mārkaṇḍeya asks for unwavering devotion to Lord Acyuta. Rudra grants him boons including eternal fame, immortality, knowledge, and the status of teacher of the Purāṇas. The chapter concludes with the glory of hearing this narrative.

story.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Vision of Rudra and Boon of Devotion
story.arc: Mārkaṇḍeya, after experiencing Nārāyaṇa's māyā, is visited by Rudra and Pārvatī. Rudra appears within his meditation, and Mārkaṇḍeya worships him. Rudra offers a boon, and Mārkaṇḍeya chooses unwavering devotion to Acyuta. Rudra grants him various boons, and Mārkaṇḍeya becomes a great devotee.
story.timeline_note: After Mārkaṇḍeya's experience of Nārāyaṇa's māyā (described in previous chapters).
story.comic_potential: Rudra's sudden appearance within Mārkaṇḍeya's heart and the sage's astonishment could be depicted humorously.

teachings.teaching: The highest gain for humans is association with saints (sādhu-samāgama).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, sādhu-samāgama corresponds to the practice of satsaṅga, which purifies the mind and accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Brāhmaṇas who are devoted to the Lord are superior to holy places and deities; they purify by mere sight.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the true guru, who embodies the divine, is more powerful than external rituals. The Kriya-Yoga practitioner seeks the grace of such a realized soul.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's māyā is inconceivable; even the wise are deluded by it.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that māyā is the creative power of the divine, and through Kriya meditation one can transcend its influence.

teachings.teaching: Devotion (bhakti) to the Lord is the highest boon, surpassing even liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's path is bhakti-oriented Kriya-Yoga, where devotion is the means and end. The practitioner seeks not liberation but loving service.

teachings.teaching: The Lord, though non-dual, appears to act through His māyā, like a dreamer.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma explains that the world is a projection of the divine consciousness; Kriya-Yoga helps realize the dream-like nature of reality.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the cosmic form of the Supreme Lord (Mahā-puruṣa) as the source of the universe, detailing how the various parts of the universe correspond to His body. It then explains the worship of the Lord through the sun's course, listing the deities presiding over each month and their roles in the cosmic order. The chapter concludes with the benefits of remembering these divine arrangements.

story.title: The Cosmic Form and the Solar Deities
story.arc: Exposition of the Lord's universal form and the monthly solar deities
story.timeline_note: Timeless; describes eternal cosmic order
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily theological and liturgical

teachings.teaching: The universe is the body of the Supreme Lord; all elements and beings are parts of His form.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the macrocosm within the microcosm of the body, where the yogi perceives the entire universe as the Lord's form during meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's ornaments and weapons symbolize cosmic principles and powers.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these are internal energies and chakras; e.g., the conch represents the sound of Om, the discus the will, etc.

teachings.teaching: Worship of the Lord through the sun's course and monthly deities purifies the mind and removes sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of solar meditation (Surya Kriya) and aligning with cosmic rhythms to purify the nadis.

teachings.teaching: Remembering the divine arrangements at dawn and dusk destroys sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the sandhyas (twilights) are optimal times for meditation and pranayama, when the mind is naturally calm.

chapter_summary: This chapter is a summary of the entire Bhagavata Purana, listing the topics covered in previous chapters, including the creation of the universe, the avatars of Vishnu, the dynasties of kings, the life of Krishna, and the teachings of bhakti-yoga. It concludes with verses glorifying the recitation of the Lord's names.

story.title: Summary of the Bhagavata Purana
story.arc: Concluding summary of the entire Purana, listing all major topics covered.
story.timeline_note: This chapter is a retrospective summary, not a narrative event.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a list of topics rather than a story.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the names of Hari (Kṛṣṇa) purifies one from all sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of nama-japa as a means to purify the mind and attain concentration.

teachings.teaching: The glory of the Lord dispels all sorrows like the sun dispels darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the light of self-knowledge (atma-jnana) removing ignorance.

teachings.teaching: Only speech that glorifies the Lord is true and auspicious; other speech is false.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on satsanga and the power of divine discourse.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge without devotion to the Lord is not beautiful, even if it is pure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses that jnana must be combined with bhakti for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Chapter 12, part 2) extols the glories of Lord Vasudeva (Krishna) and the benefits of reciting or hearing the Purana. It emphasizes that constant remembrance of Krishna's lotus feet destroys impurities and bestows purity, devotion, knowledge, and detachment. The chapter also describes the rewards for different classes of people who study the Purana, and concludes with a prayer to the supreme Lord and a salutation to Vyasa's son (Suta Goswami).

story.title: The Glories of the Bhagavata Purana
story.arc: Concluding instructions and benedictions for reciting the Purana
story.timeline_note: After the narration of the Purana to King Parikshit
story.comic_potential: A sage describing the immense spiritual benefits of reciting a text, with vivid imagery of rivers of honey and ghee.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna's lotus feet destroys all impurities and bestows auspiciousness, purity of heart, devotion to the Supreme Self, and knowledge with detachment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of constant mindfulness (smriti) of the divine, which purifies the subtle body and leads to samadhi.

teachings.teaching: Hearing or reciting even a single verse of the Bhagavata Purana with faith purifies the soul.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the power of shravana (hearing) and kirtana (chanting) as foundational practices in Kriya-Yoga for inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the Purana on specific days (dvadashi, ekadashi) or at holy places (Pushkara, Mathura, Dvaraka) grants longevity and freedom from fear.
teachings.lens_note: Aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on auspicious timings and sacred spaces to amplify spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord Hari alone is the destroyer of the sins of Kali-yuga; His glories are sung repeatedly in this Purana.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views the constant chanting of the Lord's names as a direct means to counteract the degrading influences of the age.

chapter_summary: This chapter enumerates the eighteen major Purāṇas with their verse counts, extols the Śrīmad-Bhāgavatam as the essence of all Vedānta and the supreme Purāṇa, describes its origin from Lord to Brahmā and its transmission through Nārada, Vyāsa, and Śuka, and concludes with prayers and the glory of its recitation.

story.title: The Glorification of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa
story.arc: Concluding praise of the Bhāgavata as the supreme Purāṇa, its origin and transmission, and the benefits of its study.
story.timeline_note: Post-Kurukshetra, during the recitation to the sages at Naimiṣāraṇya.
story.comic_potential: A cosmic montage of the Purāṇas as a library, with the Bhāgavata glowing as the jewel.

teachings.teaching: The Śrīmad-Bhāgavatam is the essence of all Vedānta and the supreme Purāṇa, leading to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the ultimate goal of kaivalya (liberation) through the integration of jñāna, vairāgya, and bhakti, as stated in verse 12.13.017.

teachings.teaching: Hearing, reciting, and contemplating the Bhāgavata with devotion leads to freedom from bondage.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes śravaṇa (hearing) and manana (contemplation) as key practices for inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: The Bhāgavata is the amṛta (nectar) ocean; once tasted, one finds no pleasure elsewhere.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of pratyāhāra (withdrawal of senses) and the supreme bliss of inner realization.

teachings.teaching: Giving a copy of the Bhāgavata on the full moon of Bhādrapada with a golden lion leads to the supreme goal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga may interpret this as a symbolic act of dāna (charity) that purifies the mind and accelerates spiritual progress.
